title: Hemedti Sentences Defected RSF General Al-Nour Ahmed Adam to Death in Absentia After Desert Escape to SAF
detail: RSF commander Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo (Hemedti) sentenced Major General Al-Nour Ahmed Adam — known as 'Al-Qubba' — to death in absentia on April 23, 2026, also stripping him of his military rank for desertion and collaboration with the Sudanese Armed Forces. Al-Qubba was a senior RSF commander in North Darfur who defected to the SAF following internal disputes about his exclusion from the RSF military leadership of North Darfur after the RSF's capture of El Fasher in October 2025. Al-Qubba was reportedly frustrated that Gedo Ibnshook — rather than himself — was appointed RSF commander in North Darfur, and complained of discrimination against his forces and lack of logistical support from RSF command. He then executed a dramatic desert escape, evading RSF forces across the Sahara, and reached Dongola in SAF-controlled Northern State around April 19. The death sentence signals the severity of Hemedti's concern about defections from the RSF's internal command structure, particularly from commanders with firsthand knowledge of RSF capabilities in Darfur. The episode also highlights ethnic tensions within the RSF's own ranks, with commanders from outside Hemedti's Rizeigat tribe reportedly facing discrimination in appointments and resource allocation. Al-Qubba's defection was described by military analysts as 'a major blow to the RSF' because it may provide SAF with actionable intelligence about RSF positions, personnel, and supply routes in North Darfur.

media.caption: Senior RSF commander Al-Qubba defects to SAF after desert escape from Darfur; Hemedti sentences him to death in absentia on April 23

title: Sudan's Remote Clinics Run Empty as Iran War Disrupts Global Shipping Routes and Medicine Supply Chains
detail: Reports confirmed April 23, 2026 that Sudan's already-devastated healthcare system faces a new compounding crisis: disruptions to global maritime shipping caused by the ongoing Iran war have severely impacted medicine availability in rural Sudan. Pharmacy shelves at small clinics in remote villages are running empty, with staff uncertain about when — or whether — the next medicine shipment would arrive. The disruption to Red Sea and Gulf of Aden shipping routes has added weeks to delivery times and dramatically increased costs for the limited humanitarian supply chains still reaching Sudan, affecting basic medicines including treatments for malaria, tuberculosis, and diabetes that are critical in a country where 40% of the population requires urgent medical assistance. The shipping crisis compounds the already catastrophic state of Sudan's healthcare system: WHO data records 2,052 people killed in 217+ attacks on healthcare facilities since April 2023, 37% of Sudan's facilities are non-functional, and the war in its fourth year has left millions without access to any medical care. Sudan's reliance on imported medicines — particularly for chronic diseases — means any disruption to the international supply chain has an outsized and immediate impact on the most vulnerable communities. The UN and MSF have warned that the combined effect of systematic healthcare infrastructure attacks, blocked humanitarian access, and now supply chain disruptions is creating conditions of 'medical collapse' in large parts of rural Sudan.

media.caption: Pharmacy shelves at rural clinics in Sudan begin to empty as Iran war disrupts global shipping and medicine supply chains
